# Exodus Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38v–40r (PDF pp. 73–75); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 52–53 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_012_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_012_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_012_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_012_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Institution of the Pesach — The Passover Lamb (vv. 1–14)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Mosheh and to Aharon in the land of Mitsrayim, saying:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶל־אַהֲרֹן בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם לֵאמֹר (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ve-el-Aharon be-erets Mitsrayim lemor). **Narrative shift:** For the first time in the plague narrative, YHWH speaks to both Mosheh and Aharon together *in the land of Mitsrayim* — the geographic marker is emphatic. The chapter begins a new literary unit: no longer announcement of plagues, but institution of covenant ritual. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ λέγων.

**2** "This month [shall be] for you the head of months [rosh chodashim]; it [shall be] for you the first of the months of the year.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads הַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה לָכֶם רֹאשׁ חֳדָשִׁים רִאשׁוֹן הוּא לָכֶם לְחָדְשֵׁי הַשָּׁנָה (ha-khodesh ha-zeh lakhem rosh khodashim, "this month [shall be] for you the head of months"; rishon hu lakhem le-khodshei ha-shanah, "it [shall be] first for you of the months of the year"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the re-ordering of time:** The word רֹאשׁ (rosh) = "head" — not merely "first" (rishon) but "head," implying authority and primacy. YHWH redefines the calendar: the month of redemption (Nisan/Aviv) becomes the head-month, the starting-point of Israel's year. Time itself is restructured around the act of liberation. The word חֹדֶשׁ (khodesh) = "month, new moon" — from חָדָשׁ (khadash, "new"). Every new month is a renewal. The doubled emphasis לָכֶם... לָכֶם (lakhem... lakhem, "for you... for you") makes this specifically Israel's calendar — not the Egyptian calendar, not a universal system, but "for you." LXX: ὁ μὴν οὗτος ὑμῖν ἀρχὴ μηνῶν, πρῶτός ἐστιν ὑμῖν ἐν τοῖς μησὶν τοῦ ἐνιαυτοῦ ("this month is for you the beginning of months, it is first for you among the months of the year") — ἀρχή ("beginning, origin, ruling principle") for rosh.

**3** Speak to all the congregation of Yisra'el, saying: 'On the tenth of this month, let them take for themselves, each man, a seh [lamb/kid] for a father's house, a seh for a house.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads דַּבְּרוּ אֶל־כָּל־עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר בֶּעָשֹׂר לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה וְיִקְחוּ לָהֶם אִישׁ שֶׂה לְבֵית־אָבֹת שֶׂה לַבָּיִת (dabberu el-kol-adat Yisra'el lemor, "speak to all the congregation of Yisra'el, saying"; be-asor la-khodesh ha-zeh, "on the tenth of this month"; ve-yiqkhu lahem ish seh le-vet-avot seh la-bayit, "and let them take for themselves, each man, a seh for a father's house, a seh for a house"). **Key term:** שֶׂה (seh) = "a head of small livestock" — either a lamb (כֶּבֶשׂ, keves) or a kid (עֵז, ez), as v. 5 will clarify. The word is deliberately broad. The phrase עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל (adat Yisra'el, "congregation of Yisra'el") — first occurrence of עֵדָה (edah, "congregation, assembly") for Yisra'el in Exodus. The unit of selection is בֵית־אָבֹת (vet-avot, "father's house") — the patriarchal household, the basic social unit. LXX: λαλήσατε πρὸς πᾶσαν συναγωγὴν υἱῶν Ισραηλ... λαβέτωσαν ἕκαστος πρόβατον κατ᾽ οἴκους πατριῶν ("let each one take a sheep according to the houses of families") — πρόβατον ("sheep") for seh.

**4** And if the household is too small for a seh, then he and his neighbor near to his house shall take [one] according to the number of souls; each man according to his eating you shall reckon for the seh.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאִם־יִמְעַט הַבַּיִת מִהְיוֹת מִשֶּׂה וְלָקַח הוּא וּשְׁכֵנוֹ הַקָּרֹב אֶל־בֵּיתוֹ בְּמִכְסַת נְפָשֹׁת אִישׁ לְפִי אָכְלוֹ תָּכֹסּוּ עַל־הַשֶּׂה (ve-im-yim'at ha-bayit mihyot mi-seh, "and if the household is too small from being from a seh"; ve-laqakh hu u-shkheno ha-qarov el-beto, "then he and his neighbor near to his house shall take"; be-mikhsat nefashot, "according to the number of souls"; ish lefi okhlo takhosu al-ha-seh, "each man according to his eating you shall reckon for the seh"). The verb תָּכֹסּוּ (takhosu) from כוס (kus, "to reckon, count, compute") — a rare verb. The concern is practical: the seh must be consumed entirely; no household too small should waste sacred meat. LXX: ἐὰν δὲ ὀλιγοστοὶ ὦσιν οἱ ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ ὥστε μὴ ἱκανοὺς εἶναι εἰς πρόβατον, συλλήμψεται μεθ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ τὸν γείτονα τὸν πλησίον αὐτοῦ.

**5** A seh tamim [unblemished], a male, a son of a year, it shall be for you; from the kevashim [lambs] or from the izzim [goats] you shall take [it].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שֶׂה תָמִים זָכָר בֶּן־שָׁנָה יִהְיֶה לָכֶם מִן־הַכְּבָשִׂים וּמִן־הָעִזִּים תִּקָּחוּ (seh tamim zakhar ben-shanah yihyeh lakhem, "a seh, unblemished, male, son of a year, it shall be for you"; min-ha-kevasim u-min-ha-izzim tiqqakhu, "from the lambs or from the goats you shall take"). **Key term:** תָּמִים (tamim) = "complete, whole, unblemished" — from תמם (tamam, "to be complete"). The sacrificial animal must be without defect — physical wholeness signifies spiritual integrity. The phrase בֶּן־שָׁנָה (ben-shanah, "son of a year") = one year old, in its first year. The specification מִן־הַכְּבָשִׂים וּמִן־הָעִזִּים (min-ha-kevasim u-min-ha-izzim) clarifies the earlier seh: either a lamb (keves) or a goat-kid (ez) is acceptable. LXX: πρόβατον τέλειον ἄρσεν ἐνιαύσιον ἔσται ὑμῖν· ἀπὸ τῶν ἀρνῶν καὶ τῶν ἐρίφων λήμψεσθε ("a perfect sheep, male, a year old, it shall be for you; from the lambs and from the kids you shall take") — τέλειος ("perfect, complete") for tamim.

**6** And it shall be for you for keeping until the fourteenth day of this month; and all the assembly of the congregation of Yisra'el shall slaughter it between the evenings [bein ha-arbayim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה לָכֶם לְמִשְׁמֶרֶת עַד אַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ הַזֶּה וְשָׁחֲטוּ אֹתוֹ כֹּל קְהַל עֲדַת־יִשְׂרָאֵל בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם (ve-hayah lakhem le-mishmeret ad arba'ah asar yom la-khodesh ha-zeh, "and it shall be for you for keeping until the fourteenth day of this month"; ve-shakhatu oto kol qehal adat-Yisra'el bein ha-arbayim, "and all the assembly of the congregation of Yisra'el shall slaughter it between the evenings"). **Key phrase:** בֵּין הָעַרְבָּיִם (bein ha-arbayim) = literally "between the two evenings" — a dual form. The exact timing is debated: either between sunset and full dark, or between the declining of the sun (afternoon) and sunset. The word מִשְׁמֶרֶת (mishmeret) = "keeping, charge, watch" — the animal is set aside, watched over, for four days (10th to 14th). This period of observation ensures the animal's fitness and builds anticipation. The phrase קְהַל עֲדַת־יִשְׂרָאֵל (qehal adat-Yisra'el, "assembly of the congregation of Yisra'el") is a double collective — the entire nation acts as one in this slaughter. LXX: καὶ ἔσται ὑμῖν διατετηρημένον ἕως τῆς τεσσαρεσκαιδεκάτης τοῦ μηνὸς τούτου, καὶ σφάξουσιν αὐτὸ πᾶν τὸ πλῆθος συναγωγῆς υἱῶν Ισραηλ πρὸς ἑσπέραν ("and it shall be kept for you until the fourteenth of this month, and all the multitude of the congregation of the sons of Israel shall slaughter it toward evening").

**7** And they shall take from the blood and put [it] upon the two mezuzot [doorposts] and upon the mashqof [lintel], upon the houses in which they eat it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְלָקְחוּ מִן־הַדָּם וְנָתְנוּ עַל־שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת וְעַל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף עַל הַבָּתִּים אֲשֶׁר־יֹאכְלוּ אֹתוֹ בָּהֶם (ve-laqkhu min-ha-dam ve-natnu al-shtei ha-mezuzot ve-al-ha-mashqof al ha-battim asher-yokhlu oto bahem, "and they shall take from the blood and put upon the two doorposts and upon the lintel, upon the houses in which they eat it"). **Key terms:** מְזוּזֹת (mezuzot) = "doorposts" — from זוז (zuz, "to move"); the mezuzah is the side-post of a doorframe. מַשְׁקוֹף (mashqof) = "lintel" — the upper horizontal beam above the door; from שקף (shaqaf, "to look down, overhang"). The blood marks three points of the doorway — two sides and the top — framing the entrance. The blood is applied to the exterior, visible marker of the household. LXX: καὶ λήμψονται ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος καὶ θήσουσιν ἐπὶ τῶν δύο σταθμῶν καὶ ἐπὶ τὴν φλιάν ("and they shall take from the blood and put it upon the two doorposts and upon the lintel") — σταθμός ("doorpost") for mezuzah; φλιά ("lintel") for mashqof.

**8** And they shall eat the flesh in this night, roasted of fire; and matstsot [unleavened bread] — upon merorim [bitter herbs] they shall eat it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאָכְלוּ אֶת־הַבָּשָׂר בַּלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה צְלִי־אֵשׁ וּמַצּוֹת עַל־מְרֹרִים יֹאכְלֻהוּ (ve-akhlu et-ha-basar ba-lailah ha-zeh tseli-esh u-matstsot al-merorim yokhluhu, "and they shall eat the flesh in this night, roasted of fire, and unleavened bread, upon/with bitter herbs they shall eat it"). **Key terms:** צְלִי־אֵשׁ (tseli-esh) = "roasted of fire" — not boiled, not raw, but fire-roasted. The method is emphatic: direct contact with fire. מַצּוֹת (matstsot) = "unleavened bread" — from מַצָּה (matstsah), bread without חָמֵץ (chamets, "leaven"). מְרֹרִים (merorim) = "bitter herbs" — from מָרַר (marar, "to be bitter"). The three elements of the meal: roasted flesh, unleavened bread, bitter herbs. The bitterness recalls the bitter servitude (1:14, וַיְמָרְרוּ, vayemareru, "they made bitter"). LXX: καὶ φάγονται τὰ κρέα τῇ νυκτὶ ταύτῃ· ὀπτὰ πυρὶ καὶ ἄζυμα ἐπὶ πικρίδων ἔδονται ("and they shall eat the flesh this night; roasted with fire and unleavened bread upon bitter herbs they shall eat") — ἄζυμα ("unleavened") for matstsot; πικρίδες ("bitter herbs") for merorim.

**9** Do not eat from it raw [na], or boiled [bashel] — boiled in water [mevushal ba-mayim]; but [only] roasted of fire — its head with its legs and with its inner parts.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אַל־תֹּאכְלוּ מִמֶּנּוּ נָא וּבָשֵׁל מְבֻשָּׁל בַּמָּיִם כִּי אִם־צְלִי־אֵשׁ רֹאשׁוֹ עַל־כְּרָעָיו וְעַל־קִרְבּוֹ (al-tokhlu mimmennu na u-vashel mevushal ba-mayim ki im-tseli-esh rosho al-kera'av ve-al-qirbo). **Key term:** נָא (na) here = "raw, underdone" — distinct from the particle of entreaty נָא. The prohibition is threefold: not raw, not boiled, only fire-roasted. The animal is roasted whole — head, legs, inner parts — preserving its unity. The word קִרְבּוֹ (qirbo) = "its inner parts, entrails." LXX: οὐκ ἔδεσθε ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ὠμὸν οὐδὲ ἡψημένον ἐν ὕδατι, ἀλλ᾽ ὀπτὰ πυρί, κεφαλὴν σὺν τοῖς ποσὶν καὶ τοῖς ἐνδοσθίοις ("you shall not eat of them raw nor boiled in water, but roasted with fire, head with the feet and the inward parts") — ὠμός ("raw") for na.

**10** And you shall not leave over from it until morning; and that which remains from it until morning — in fire you shall burn [it].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְלֹא־תוֹתִירוּ מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר וְהַנֹּתָר מִמֶּנּוּ עַד־בֹּקֶר בָּאֵשׁ תִּשְׂרֹפוּ (ve-lo-totiru mimmennu ad-boqer ve-ha-notar mimmennu ad-boqer ba-esh tisrofu, "and you shall not leave over from it until morning; and that which remains from it until morning — in fire you shall burn"). Nothing of the pesach offering survives the night — the meal is complete, the consumption total. What cannot be eaten is burned. LXX: οὐκ ἀπολείψετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἕως πρωΐ καὶ ὀστοῦν οὐ συντρίψετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ· τὰ δὲ καταλειπόμενα ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἕως πρωΐ ἐν πυρὶ κατακαύσετε — note LXX inserts the bone-prohibition here (from v. 46 in MT).

**11** And thus you shall eat it: your loins girded, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand; and you shall eat it in chipazon [haste] — it [is] pesach [passover] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכָכָה תֹּאכְלוּ אֹתוֹ מָתְנֵיכֶם חֲגֻרִים נַעֲלֵיכֶם בְּרַגְלֵיכֶם וּמַקֶּלְכֶם בְּיֶדְכֶם וַאֲכַלְתֶּם אֹתוֹ בְּחִפָּזוֹן פֶּסַח הוּא לַיהוָה (ve-khakhah tokhlu oto motnekhem khagurim na'aleikhem be-ragleikhem u-maqqelkhem be-yedkhem va-akhaltem oto be-khippazon pesakh hu la-YHWH, "and thus you shall eat it: your loins girded, your sandals on your feet, and your staff in your hand; and you shall eat it in haste — it [is] a pesach to YHWH"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the posture of departure:** Three details paint a portrait of readiness: (1) מָתְנֵיכֶם חֲגֻרִים (motnekhem khagurim) — loins girded, the long robe tucked up for rapid movement; (2) נַעֲלֵיכֶם בְּרַגְלֵיכֶם (na'aleikhem be-ragleikhem) — sandals on feet, ready to walk; (3) מַקֶּלְכֶם בְּיֶדְכֶם (maqqelkhem be-yedkhem) — staff in hand, the traveler's implement. The word חִפָּזוֹן (khippazon) = "haste, hurry, trepidation" — from חפז (khafaz, "to hurry, be in alarm"). This rare word appears only three times in the OT (here, Deut 16:3, Isa 52:12). It connotes not mere speed but urgent, anxious haste — the haste of escape. **Key term:** פֶּסַח (pesakh) = "passover" — the name of the offering and the festival. From פסח (pasakh, "to pass over, skip, protect"). The etymology will be explained in v. 13 and v. 23. LXX: οὕτως δὲ φάγεσθε αὐτό· αἱ ὀσφύες ὑμῶν περιεζωσμέναι, καὶ τὰ ὑποδήματα ἐν τοῖς ποσὶν ὑμῶν, καὶ αἱ βακτηρίαι ἐν ταῖς χερσὶν ὑμῶν· καὶ ἔδεσθε αὐτὸ μετὰ σπουδῆς· πάσχα ἐστὶν κυρίῳ ("thus you shall eat it: your loins girded, and sandals on your feet, and staffs in your hands; and you shall eat it with haste — it is pascha to the Lord") — σπουδή ("haste, urgency") for khippazon; πάσχα (pascha) transliterating pesakh.

**12** And I will pass through [avarti] the land of Mitsrayim in this night, and I will strike every bekhor [firstborn] in the land of Mitsrayim, from adam [man] unto behemah [beast]; and against all the gods [elohei] of Mitsrayim I will execute shefatim [judgments] — I [am] YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָבַרְתִּי בְאֶרֶץ־מִצְרַיִם בַּלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה וְהִכֵּיתִי כָל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מֵאָדָם וְעַד־בְּהֵמָה וּבְכָל־אֱלֹהֵי מִצְרַיִם אֶעֱשֶׂה שְׁפָטִים אֲנִי יְהוָה (ve-avarti ve-erets-Mitsrayim ba-lailah ha-zeh, "and I will pass through the land of Mitsrayim in this night"; ve-hikkeiti khol-bekhor be-erets Mitsrayim me-adam ve-ad-behemah, "and I will strike every firstborn in the land of Mitsrayim, from man unto beast"; u-ve-khol-elohei Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim, "and against all the gods of Mitsrayim I will execute judgments"; ani YHWH, "I [am] YHWH"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — divine warfare against the gods:** The phrase וּבְכָל־אֱלֹהֵי מִצְרַיִם אֶעֱשֶׂה שְׁפָטִים (u-ve-khol-elohei Mitsrayim e'eseh shefatim) = "and against all the gods of Mitsrayim I will execute judgments." The plagues are not merely punishment of Par'oh but a systematic judgment against the Egyptian pantheon. The word שְׁפָטִים (shefatim) = "judgments, acts of justice" — from שפט (shafat, "to judge"). YHWH judges the gods themselves — their powerlessness is exposed. The closing אֲנִי יְהוָה (ani YHWH, "I am YHWH") is a self-identification formula of sovereignty. The verb עָבַרְתִּי (avarti) = "I will pass through" — from עבר (avar), distinct from פסח (pasakh, "to pass over"). YHWH passes *through* to strike, but passes *over* the blood-marked houses. LXX: καὶ ἐλεύσομαι ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ἐν τῇ νυκτὶ ταύτῃ καὶ πατάξω πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ἀπὸ ἀνθρώπου ἕως κτήνους, καὶ ἐν πᾶσιν τοῖς θεοῖς τῶν Αἰγυπτίων ποιήσω τὴν ἐκδίκησιν· ἐγὼ κύριος ("and I will come in the land of Egypt in this night and strike every firstborn... and against all the gods of the Egyptians I will execute vengeance — I am the Lord") — ἐκδίκησις ("vengeance") for shefatim.

**13** And the blood shall be for you for a sign [le-ot] upon the houses where you [are]; and I will see the blood and I will pasakh [pass over/protect] over you, and there shall not be among you a negef [plague/blow] for destruction [le-mashchit] when I strike in the land of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה הַדָּם לָכֶם לְאֹת עַל הַבָּתִּים אֲשֶׁר אַתֶּם שָׁם וְרָאִיתִי אֶת־הַדָּם וּפָסַחְתִּי עֲלֵכֶם וְלֹא־יִהְיֶה בָכֶם נֶגֶף לְמַשְׁחִית בְּהַכֹּתִי בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (ve-hayah ha-dam lakhem le-ot al ha-battim asher attem sham, "and the blood shall be for you for a sign upon the houses where you [are]"; ve-ra'iti et-ha-dam u-fasakhti aleikhem, "and I will see the blood and I will pass over you"; ve-lo-yihyeh vakhem negef le-mashkhit be-hakkoti be-erets Mitsrayim, "and there shall not be among you a plague for destruction when I strike in the land of Mitsrayim"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the blood-sign and the pasakh:** The verb וּפָסַחְתִּי (u-fasakhti) from פסח (pasakh) — the core verb of the Passover. Its meaning is debated: (a) "pass over, skip" — YHWH skips the marked houses; (b) "protect, stand guard over" — from an Akkadian cognate, YHWH stations himself at the door to prevent the destroyer from entering (cf. v. 23). The blood is לְאֹת (le-ot, "for a sign") — the same word used for the rainbow (Bereshit 9:12) and circumcision (Bereshit 17:11). It is a covenant marker. The word מַשְׁחִית (mashkhit) = "destroyer, destruction" — from שחת (shakhat, "to destroy, corrupt"). This is the destroying agent, the force of death that YHWH unleashes. The blood does not make the house invisible — YHWH sees (וְרָאִיתִי, ve-ra'iti, "and I will see") the blood and actively protects. LXX: καὶ ἔσται τὸ αἷμα ὑμῖν σημεῖον ἐπὶ τῶν οἰκιῶν ἐν αἷς ὑμεῖς ἐστε ἐκεῖ, καὶ ὄψομαι τὸ αἷμα καὶ σκεπάσω ὑμᾶς ("and the blood shall be a sign for you upon the houses where you are, and I will see the blood and I will cover/shelter you") — σκεπάζω ("cover, shelter, protect") for pasakh — supporting the "protect" interpretation.

**14** And this day shall be for you for a remembrance [le-zikkaron]; and you shall celebrate it [as] a chag [feast] to YHWH — throughout your generations [as] a chuqqat olam [statute of eternity] you shall celebrate it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה לָכֶם לְזִכָּרוֹן וְחַגֹּתֶם אֹתוֹ חַג לַיהוָה לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם תְּחָגֻּהוּ (ve-hayah ha-yom ha-zeh lakhem le-zikkaron ve-khagotem oto khag la-YHWH le-doroteikhem khuqqat olam tekhaguhu). **Key terms:** זִכָּרוֹן (zikkaron) = "remembrance, memorial" — from זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). This is not passive memory but active re-enactment. חַג (khag) = "feast, festival" — from חגג (khagag, "to celebrate, make pilgrimage"). חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (khuqqat olam) = "statute of eternity" — from חֹק (khoq, "decree, statute") + עוֹלָם (olam, "eternity, perpetuity"). The Passover is instituted as permanent, trans-generational obligation. LXX: καὶ ἔσται ἡ ἡμέρα ὑμῖν αὕτη μνημόσυνον, καὶ ἑορτάσετε αὐτὴν ἑορτὴν κυρίῳ εἰς πάσας τὰς γενεὰς ὑμῶν· νόμιμον αἰώνιον ἑορτάσετε αὐτήν.

## The Feast of Matstsot — Unleavened Bread (vv. 15–20)

**15** Seven days you shall eat matstsot [unleavened bread]; surely on the first day you shall cause to cease [tashbitu] se'or [leaven] from your houses; for anyone eating chamets [leavened bread] — that nefesh [soul] shall be cut off [ve-nikhretah] from Yisra'el, from the first day until the seventh day.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שִׁבְעַת יָמִים מַצּוֹת תֹּאכֵלוּ אַךְ בַּיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן תַּשְׁבִּיתוּ שְּׂאֹר מִבָּתֵּיכֶם כִּי כָּל־אֹכֵל חָמֵץ וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל מִיּוֹם הָרִאשֹׁן עַד־יוֹם הַשְּׁבִעִי (shiv'at yamim matstsot tokhelu, "seven days unleavened bread you shall eat"; akh ba-yom ha-rishon tashbitu se'or mi-batteikhem, "surely on the first day you shall cause to cease leaven from your houses"; ki khol-okhel chamets ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi mi-Yisra'el, "for anyone eating leavened bread — that soul shall be cut off from Yisra'el"). **Key terms:** שְׂאֹר (se'or) = "leaven, sourdough" — the fermenting agent itself, distinct from חָמֵץ (chamets) = "leavened bread" — the product made with leaven. The verb תַּשְׁבִּיתוּ (tashbitu) = hiphil of שבת (shavat, "to cease") — causative: "you shall cause to cease," i.e., remove entirely. The penalty וְנִכְרְתָה (ve-nikhretah) = niphal of כרת (karat, "to cut off") — the karet penalty, excision from the community, the most severe communal sanction. LXX: ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ἄζυμα ἔδεσθε, ἀπὸ δὲ τῆς ἡμέρας τῆς πρώτης ἀφανιεῖτε ζύμην ἐκ τῶν οἰκιῶν ὑμῶν.

**16** And on the first day [there shall be] a miqra-qodesh [holy convocation], and on the seventh day [there shall be] a miqra-qodesh for you; no melakhah [work] shall be done on them, except [akh] what is eaten by every nefesh [soul] — that alone shall be done by you.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבַיּוֹם הָרִאשׁוֹן מִקְרָא־קֹדֶשׁ וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי מִקְרָא־קֹדֶשׁ יִהְיֶה לָכֶם כָּל־מְלָאכָה לֹא־יֵעָשֶׂה בָהֶם אַךְ אֲשֶׁר יֵאָכֵל לְכָל־נֶפֶשׁ הוּא לְבַדּוֹ יֵעָשֶׂה לָכֶם. **Key term:** מִקְרָא־קֹדֶשׁ (miqra-qodesh) = "holy convocation" — from קרא (qara, "to call, read, proclaim") + קֹדֶשׁ (qodesh, "holiness"). A proclaimed gathering set apart as holy. מְלָאכָה (melakhah) = "work, skilled labor" — the same word used for the Shabbat prohibition. LXX: καὶ ἡ ἡμέρα ἡ πρώτη κληθήσεται ἁγία, καὶ ἡ ἡμέρα ἡ ἑβδόμη κλητὴ ἁγία ἔσται ὑμῖν.

**17** And you shall keep the matstsot, for on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh] I brought out your hosts [tsiv'oteikhem] from the land of Mitsrayim; and you shall keep this day throughout your generations [as] a chuqqat olam [statute of eternity].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַמַּצּוֹת כִּי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה הוֹצֵאתִי אֶת־צִבְאוֹתֵיכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (u-shemartem et-ha-matstsot ki be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh hotseiti et-tsiv'oteikhem me-erets Mitsrayim, "and you shall keep the unleavened bread, for on this very day I brought out your hosts from the land of Mitsrayim"). The phrase בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh, "on the bone/substance of this day") = "on this very day" — the word עֶצֶם (etsem) = "bone, substance, self" — emphasizing the precise day. The word צִבְאוֹתֵיכֶם (tsiv'oteikhem) = "your hosts/armies" — from צָבָא (tsava, "army, host"). Yisra'el departs not as a rabble of slaves but as organized hosts — a military metaphor for the redeemed people. LXX: καὶ φυλάξεσθε τὴν ἐντολὴν ταύτην.

**18** In the first [month], on the fourteenth day of the month, in the evening [ba-erev], you shall eat matstsot, until the twenty-first day of the month, in the evening.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בָּרִאשֹׁן בְּאַרְבָּעָה עָשָׂר יוֹם לַחֹדֶשׁ בָּעֶרֶב תֹּאכְלוּ מַצֹּת עַד יוֹם הָאֶחָד וְעֶשְׂרִים לַחֹדֶשׁ בָּעָרֶב (ba-rishon be-arba'ah asar yom la-khodesh ba-erev tokhlu matstsot ad yom ha-ekhad ve-esrim la-khodesh ba-arev). The precise calendar: from the 14th evening to the 21st evening — seven full days of matstsot. LXX: ἐναρχομένου τῇ τεσσαρεσκαιδεκάτῃ ἡμέρᾳ τοῦ μηνὸς τοῦ πρώτου ἀφ᾽ ἑσπέρας ἔδεσθε ἄζυμα ἕως ἡμέρας μιᾶς καὶ εἰκάδος τοῦ μηνός, ἕως ἑσπέρας.

**19** Seven days se'or [leaven] shall not be found in your houses; for anyone eating machmetset [leavened thing] — that nefesh [soul] shall be cut off from the congregation of Yisra'el, whether a ger [sojourner] or a native of the land [ezrach ha-arets].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שִׁבְעַת יָמִים שְׂאֹר לֹא יִמָּצֵא בְּבָתֵּיכֶם כִּי כָּל־אֹכֵל מַחְמֶצֶת וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מֵעֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל בַּגֵּר וּבְאֶזְרַח הָאָרֶץ (shiv'at yamim se'or lo yimmatse be-vatteikhem, "seven days leaven shall not be found in your houses"; ki khol-okhel makhmetset, "for anyone eating a leavened thing"; ve-nikhretah ha-nefesh ha-hi me-adat Yisra'el, "that soul shall be cut off from the congregation of Yisra'el"; ba-ger u-ve-ezrakh ha-arets, "whether a sojourner or a native of the land"). **Key terms:** מַחְמֶצֶת (makhmetset) = "a leavened thing" — from chamets. גֵּר (ger) = "sojourner, resident alien" — one who lives among Yisra'el without being born into it. אֶזְרָח (ezrakh) = "native, native-born" — from זרח (zarakh, "to rise, shine"). The karet penalty applies equally to ger and native — the first statement of legal equality between native and sojourner in Torah. LXX: ἑπτὰ ἡμέρας ζύμη οὐχ εὑρεθήσεται ἐν ταῖς οἰκίαις ὑμῶν.

**20** Every machmetset [leavened thing] you shall not eat; in all your dwelling-places [moshevoteikhem] you shall eat matstsot."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כָּל־מַחְמֶצֶת לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ בְּכֹל מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם תֹּאכְלוּ מַצּוֹת (kol-makhmetset lo tokhelu be-khol moshevoteikhem tokhlu matstsot). The word מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם (moshevoteikhem) = "your dwelling-places" — from ישב (yashav, "to sit, dwell"). The law applies universally in all locations. LXX: πᾶν ζυμωτὸν οὐκ ἔδεσθε, ἐν παντὶ δὲ κατοικητηρίῳ ὑμῶν ἔδεσθε ἄζυμα.

## Mosheh Instructs the Elders — The Blood Rite (vv. 21–28)

**21** And Mosheh called to all the elders [ziqnei] of Yisra'el and said to them: "Draw out [mishkhu] and take for yourselves tso'n [flock-animals] according to your families, and slaughter the pesach.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְכָל־זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם מִשְׁכוּ וּקְחוּ לָכֶם צֹאן לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתֵיכֶם וְשַׁחֲטוּ הַפָּסַח (vayiqra Mosheh le-khol-ziqnei Yisra'el vayomer alehem mishkhu u-qkhu lakhem tso'n le-mishpekhotekhem ve-shakhatu ha-pasakh). **Key verb:** מִשְׁכוּ (mishkhu) = "draw out, pull" — from משך (mashakh, "to draw, drag, pull"). The verb may imply drawing the animal from the Egyptian flock, or drawing it out from one's own animals. Some interpreters see a secondary meaning: "withdraw [from idolatry]" — the lamb was sacred to Egyptian religion, and slaughtering it was an act of separation. LXX: ἐκάλεσεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πᾶσαν γερουσίαν υἱῶν Ισραηλ... ἀπελθόντες λάβετε ὑμῖν ἑαυτοῖς πρόβατον κατὰ συγγενείας ὑμῶν καὶ θύσατε τὸ πάσχα.

**22** And you shall take a bundle of ezov [hyssop] and dip [it] in the blood that [is] in the basin [ba-saf], and you shall touch [higatem] the mashqof [lintel] and the two mezuzot [doorposts] with the blood that [is] in the basin; and you — no man [ish] shall go out from the door of his house until morning.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּלְקַחְתֶּם אֲגֻדַּת אֵזוֹב וּטְבַלְתֶּם בַּדָּם אֲשֶׁר־בַּסָּף וְהִגַּעְתֶּם אֶל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף וְאֶל־שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת מִן־הַדָּם אֲשֶׁר בַּסָּף וְאַתֶּם לֹא תֵצְאוּ אִישׁ מִפֶּתַח־בֵּיתוֹ עַד־בֹּקֶר (u-leqakhtem agudat ezov u-tevaltem ba-dam asher-ba-saf ve-higatem el-ha-mashqof ve-el-shtei ha-mezuzot min-ha-dam asher ba-saf ve-attem lo tets'u ish mi-petakh-beto ad-boqer). **Key terms:** אֲגֻדָּה (agudah) = "bundle, bunch" — from אגד (agad, "to bind, tie"). אֵזוֹב (ezov) = "hyssop" — a small, bushy plant used for ritual sprinkling (cf. Lev 14:4, Num 19:6, Ps 51:9). סַף (saf) = "basin, threshold" — the dual meaning is significant: the blood collected in a basin at the threshold/doorstep. The verb וּטְבַלְתֶּם (u-tevaltem) from טבל (taval, "to dip") — the hyssop is dipped in the blood and applied to the door-frame. The prohibition against leaving until morning: the blood protects only those inside the marked house. LXX: λήμψεσθε δὲ δεσμὴν ὑσσώπου καὶ βάψαντες ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος τοῦ παρὰ τὴν θύραν καθίξετε τῆς φλιᾶς καὶ ἐπ᾽ ἀμφοτέρων τῶν σταθμῶν ("you shall take a bundle of hyssop and having dipped from the blood which is by the door, you shall touch the lintel and upon both doorposts") — ὕσσωπος ("hyssop") for ezov.

**23** And YHWH will pass through [ve-avar] to strike Mitsrayim, and He will see the blood upon the mashqof [lintel] and upon the two mezuzot [doorposts], and YHWH will pasakh [pass over/protect] over the door, and He will not allow the mashchit [destroyer] to come into your houses to strike.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָבַר יְהוָה לִנְגֹּף אֶת־מִצְרַיִם וְרָאָה אֶת־הַדָּם עַל־הַמַּשְׁקוֹף וְעַל שְׁתֵּי הַמְּזוּזֹת וּפָסַח יְהוָה עַל־הַפֶּתַח וְלֹא יִתֵּן הַמַּשְׁחִית לָבֹא אֶל־בָּתֵּיכֶם לִנְגֹּף (ve-avar YHWH lingof et-Mitsrayim ve-ra'ah et-ha-dam al-ha-mashqof ve-al shtei ha-mezuzot u-fasakh YHWH al-ha-petakh ve-lo yitten ha-mashkhit lavo el-batteikhem lingof, "and YHWH will pass through to strike Mitsrayim, and He will see the blood upon the lintel and upon the two doorposts, and YHWH will pass over the door, and He will not allow the destroyer to come into your houses to strike"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the pasakh and the mashchit:** Two distinct actions: (1) וְעָבַר (ve-avar) — YHWH "passes through" (avar) the land to strike; (2) וּפָסַח (u-fasakh) — YHWH "passes over / protects" (pasakh) the marked door. The verb pasakh here has its fullest meaning: YHWH stations himself at the door (עַל־הַפֶּתַח, al-ha-petakh, "over the door/entrance") as a guardian, not permitting the מַשְׁחִית (mashkhit, "destroyer") to enter. The mashkhit is a distinct destructive agent — whether an angel, a force, or a personified death — that YHWH unleashes but also restrains. The verb יִתֵּן (yitten) = "will give, allow, permit" — YHWH actively prevents the destroyer's entry. This is not passive exemption but active, personal protection. LXX: καὶ παρελεύσεται κύριος πατάξαι τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους καὶ ὄψεται τὸ αἷμα ἐπὶ τῆς φλιᾶς καὶ ἐπ᾽ ἀμφοτέρων τῶν σταθμῶν, καὶ παρελεύσεται κύριος τὴν θύραν καὶ οὐκ ἀφήσει τὸν ὀλεθρεύοντα εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὰς οἰκίας ὑμῶν πατάξαι ("and the Lord will pass by to strike the Egyptians, and will see the blood upon the lintel and upon both doorposts, and the Lord will pass by the door and will not allow the destroyer to enter your houses to strike") — ὀλεθρεύων ("the one destroying") for mashkhit.

**24** And you shall keep this word [davar] as a chuqqah [statute] for you and for your sons unto olam [eternity].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה לְחָק־לְךָ וּלְבָנֶיךָ עַד־עוֹלָם (u-shemartem et-ha-davar ha-zeh le-khoq-lekha u-le-vaneicha ad-olam). **26** And it shall be, when your sons say to you: 'What [is] this avodah [service] to you?' —

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיָה כִּי־יֹאמְרוּ אֲלֵיכֶם בְּנֵיכֶם מָה הָעֲבֹדָה הַזֹּאת לָכֶם (ve-hayah ki-yomru aleikhem beneikhem mah ha-avodah ha-zot lakhem). > **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַאֲמַרְתֶּם זֶבַח־פֶּסַח הוּא לַיהוָה אֲשֶׁר פָּסַח עַל־בָּתֵּי בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּמִצְרַיִם בְּנָגְפּוֹ אֶת־מִצְרַיִם וְאֶת־בָּתֵּינוּ הִצִּיל וַיִּקֹּד הָעָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲוּוּ (va-amartem zevakh-pesakh hu la-YHWH asher pasakh al-batei venei-Yisra'el be-Mitsrayim be-nogfo et-Mitsrayim ve-et-batteinu hitstsil vayiqqod ha-am vayishtakhavu). **Key terms:** זֶבַח (zevakh) = "sacrifice" — from זבח (zavakh, "to slaughter, sacrifice"). The response of the people: וַיִּקֹּד (vayiqqod, "bowed the head") + וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲוּוּ (vayishtakhavu, "prostrated themselves") — a double gesture of reverent worship. **28** And the sons of Yisra'el went and did [it]; as YHWH commanded Mosheh and Aharon, so they did.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּלְכוּ וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן כֵּן עָשׂוּ (vayelkhu vaya'asu benei Yisra'el ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh ve-Aharon ken asu). The compliance formula: "as YHWH commanded... so they did" — obedience frames the action. LXX: καὶ ἀπελθόντες ἐποίησαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ καθὰ ἐνετείλατο κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ καὶ Ααρων, οὕτως ἐποίησαν.

## The Tenth Plague — Death of the Firstborn (vv. 29–36)

**29** And it was at the middle of the night [ba-khatsi ha-lailah], and YHWH struck every bekhor [firstborn] in the land of Mitsrayim, from the bekhor of Par'oh sitting upon his throne unto the bekhor of the captive [ha-shevi] who [was] in the house of the pit [beit ha-bor], and every bekhor of beast [behemah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בַּחֲצִי הַלַּיְלָה וַיהוָה הִכָּה כָל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכֹר פַּרְעֹה הַיֹּשֵׁב עַל־כִּסְאוֹ עַד בְּכוֹר הַשְּׁבִי אֲשֶׁר בְּבֵית הַבּוֹר וְכֹל בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה (vayehi ba-khatsi ha-lailah va-YHWH hikkah khol-bekhor be-erets Mitsrayim mi-bekhor Par'oh ha-yoshev al-kis'o ad bekhor ha-shevi asher be-veit ha-bor ve-khol bekhor behemah, "and it was at the middle of the night, and YHWH struck every firstborn in the land of Mitsrayim, from the firstborn of Par'oh sitting upon his throne unto the firstborn of the captive who was in the house of the pit, and every firstborn of beast"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the execution of the tenth plague:** Note the shift from the announcement in 11:5 (where the lowest was "the slave-woman behind the mill") to the fulfillment here (where the lowest is "the captive in the pit/dungeon"). The שְּׁבִי (shevi) = "captive, prisoner" — one even lower than a slave. The word בּוֹר (bor) = "pit, cistern, dungeon." The execution matches the prediction but with variation — the narrator reports the actual scope. The verb הִכָּה (hikkah) = hiphil of נכה (nakhah, "to strike") — YHWH is the explicit subject: YHWH struck. LXX: ἐγενήθη δὲ μεσούσης τῆς νυκτὸς καὶ κύριος ἐπάταξεν πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ, ἀπὸ πρωτοτόκου Φαραω τοῦ καθημένου ἐπὶ τοῦ θρόνου ἕως πρωτοτόκου τῆς αἰχμαλωτίδος τῆς ἐν τῷ λάκκῳ ("and it happened at midnight and the Lord struck every firstborn in the land of Egypt, from the firstborn of Pharao sitting on the throne to the firstborn of the captive woman in the pit").

**30** And Par'oh rose in the night — he, and all his servants, and all Mitsrayim — and there was a tse'aqah gedolah [great cry] in Mitsrayim, for there was not a house where there [was] not [one] dead.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּקָם פַּרְעֹה לַיְלָה הוּא וְכָל־עֲבָדָיו וְכָל־מִצְרַיִם וַתְּהִי צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה בְּמִצְרָיִם כִּי־אֵין בַּיִת אֲשֶׁר אֵין־שָׁם מֵת (vayaqam Par'oh lailah hu ve-khol-avadav ve-khol-Mitsrayim vatehi tse'aqah gedolah be-Mitsrayim ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met). The fulfillment of 11:6: the צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה (tse'aqah gedolah, "great cry") that was predicted now erupts. The phrase כִּי־אֵין בַּיִת אֲשֶׁר אֵין־שָׁם מֵת (ki-ein bayit asher ein-sham met, "for there was not a house where there was not one dead") — the double negative intensifies: no exception, every house touched by death. Par'oh, who would not release Yisra'el, now rises (וַיָּקָם, vayaqam) in the night — the verb of urgency. LXX: καὶ ἀναστὰς Φαραω νυκτὸς... καὶ ἐγενήθη κραυγὴ μεγάλη ἐν πάσῃ γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ· οὐ γὰρ ἦν οἰκία ἐν ᾗ οὐκ ἦν ἐν αὐτῇ τεθνηκώς.

**31** And he called to Mosheh and to Aharon [by] night and said: "Rise! Go out from the midst of my people, both you and the sons of Yisra'el! And go! Serve [ivdu] YHWH as you spoke!

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקְרָא לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְאַהֲרֹן לַיְלָה וַיֹּאמֶר קוּמוּ צְּאוּ מִתּוֹךְ עַמִּי גַּם־אַתֶּם גַּם־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּלְכוּ עִבְדוּ אֶת־יְהוָה כְּדַבֶּרְכֶם (vayiqra le-Mosheh u-le-Aharon lailah vayomer qumu tse'u mi-tokh ammi gam-attem gam-benei Yisra'el u-lekhu ivdu et-YHWH ke-dabberkem, "and he called to Mosheh and to Aharon by night and said: rise, go out from the midst of my people, both you and the sons of Yisra'el, and go, serve YHWH as you spoke"). The rapid-fire imperatives: קוּמוּ (qumu, "rise!") — צְּאוּ (tse'u, "go out!") — לְכוּ (lekhu, "go!") — עִבְדוּ (ivdu, "serve!"). Par'oh, who refused to let them serve YHWH (5:1-2), now commands it. The phrase כְּדַבֶּרְכֶם (ke-dabberkem, "as you spoke") = unconditional surrender — no conditions, no negotiations, exactly as Mosheh demanded. LXX: καὶ ἐκάλεσεν Φαραω Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων νυκτὸς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς· ἀνάστητε καὶ ἐξέλθατε ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ μου... καὶ βαδίζετε λατρεύσατε κυρίῳ τῷ θεῷ ὑμῶν καθάπερ λέγετε.

**32** Also your tso'n [flocks] and also your baqar [herds] — take [them] as you spoke! And go! And barekhu [bless] also me!"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads גַּם־צֹאנְכֶם גַּם־בְּקַרְכֶם קְחוּ כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתֶּם וָלֵכוּ וּבֵרַכְתֶּם גַּם־אֹתִי (gam-tsonkhem gam-beqarkhem qekhu ka-asher dibbartem va-lekhu u-verakhtem gam-oti, "also your flocks and also your herds — take as you spoke! And go! And bless also me!"). **Key phrase — u-verakhtem gam-oti:** Par'oh's final word to Mosheh is a plea: וּבֵרַכְתֶּם גַּם־אֹתִי (u-verakhtem gam-oti, "and bless also me!"). The verb בֵּרַכְתֶּם (verakhtem) from ברך (barakh, "to bless"). The man who said "Who is YHWH?" (5:2) now begs for YHWH's blessing. The word גַּם (gam, "also, even") is poignant — "even me," as if Par'oh recognizes he is the last person entitled to ask. LXX: καὶ τὰ πρόβατα καὶ τοὺς βόας ὑμῶν ἀναλαβόντες πορεύεσθε, εὐλογήσατε δὴ κἀμέ ("taking your sheep and your cattle, go — and bless me also") — εὐλογέω ("bless") for barakh.

**33** And Mitsrayim was urgent [vateckezaq] upon the people, to hasten to send them out from the land, for they said: "We [are] all dead [men] [kullanu metim]!"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַתֶּחֱזַק מִצְרַיִם עַל־הָעָם לְמַהֵר לְשַׁלְּחָם מִן־הָאָרֶץ כִּי אָמְרוּ כֻּלָּנוּ מֵתִים (vateckezaq Mitsrayim al-ha-am le-maher le-shalkham min-ha-arets ki amru kullanu metim, "and Mitsrayim was urgent upon the people to hasten to send them out from the land, for they said: we [are] all dead"). The verb וַתֶּחֱזַק (vateckezaq) from חזק (chazaq, "to be strong, urgent") — the same root used for the hardening of Par'oh's heart! Now the Egyptians' urgency (chazaq) drives the release. The cry כֻּלָּנוּ מֵתִים (kullanu metim, "we are all dead") — they speak of themselves as already dead, anticipating more destruction. LXX: καὶ κατεβιάζοντο οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι τὸν λαὸν σπουδῇ ἐκβαλεῖν αὐτοὺς ἐκ τῆς γῆς· εἶπαν γὰρ ὅτι πάντες ἡμεῖς ἀποθνῄσκομεν.

**34** And the people carried their dough before it was leavened [terem yechmats], their misharot [kneading-troughs] bound up in their garments upon their shoulders.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּשָּׂא הָעָם אֶת־בְּצֵקוֹ טֶרֶם יֶחְמָץ מִשְׁאֲרֹתָם צְרֻרֹת בְּשִׂמְלֹתָם עַל־שִׁכְמָם (vayissa ha-am et-betseqo terem yechmats mish'arotam tserurot be-simlotam al-shikhmam, "and the people carried their dough before it was leavened, their kneading-troughs bound up in their garments upon their shoulders"). **Key terms:** בָּצֵק (batseq) = "dough." The verb יֶחְמָץ (yechmats) from חמץ (chamats, "to be leavened, ferment") — the dough had not yet fermented. מִשְׁאֲרֹת (mish'arot) = "kneading-troughs" — from שאר (likely related to se'or, "leaven"). This is the narrative origin of matstsot: unleavened bread exists because the departure was too hasty for the dough to rise. LXX: καὶ ἀνέλαβεν ὁ λαὸς τὸ σταῖς πρὸ τοῦ ζυμωθῆναι, τὰ φυράματα αὐτῶν ἐνδεδεμένα ἐν τοῖς ἱματίοις αὐτῶν ἐπὶ τῶν ὤμων.

**35** And the sons of Yisra'el did according to the word of Mosheh; and they asked [vayish'alu] from Mitsrayim articles of silver and articles of gold and garments.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל עָשׂוּ כִּדְבַר מֹשֶׁה וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּשְׂמָלֹת (u-venei-Yisra'el asu ki-dvar Mosheh vayish'alu mi-Mitsrayim kelei-khesef u-khlei zahav u-semalot). The fulfillment of 11:2 — the verb וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ (vayish'alu) from sha'al ("to ask, request") — they asked and received. The addition of שְׂמָלֹת (semalot, "garments") goes beyond the silver and gold mentioned in 11:2. LXX: οἱ δὲ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐποίησαν καθὰ συνέταξεν αὐτοῖς Μωυσῆς, καὶ ᾔτησαν παρὰ τῶν Αἰγυπτίων σκεύη ἀργυρᾶ καὶ χρυσᾶ καὶ ἱματισμόν.

**36** And YHWH gave the chen [favor] of the people in the eyes of Mitsrayim, and they let them ask [vayash'ilum]; and they despoiled [vayenatselu] Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיהוָה נָתַן אֶת־חֵן הָעָם בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרַיִם וַיַּשְׁאִלוּם וַיְנַצְּלוּ אֶת־מִצְרָיִם (va-YHWH natan et-khen ha-am be-einei Mitsrayim vayash'ilum vayenattselu et-Mitsrayim, "and YHWH gave the favor of the people in the eyes of Mitsrayim, and they granted their requests, and they despoiled Mitsrayim"). **Key verb:** וַיְנַצְּלוּ (vayenattselu) = piel of נצל (natsal, "to strip, plunder, despoil") — the same root as "deliver" (hiphil hitstsil) but in piel means "to strip bare, plunder." The slaves leave as victors, stripping the wealth of their oppressors. This fulfills Bereshit 15:14. LXX: καὶ κύριος ἔδωκεν τὴν χάριν τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ ἐναντίον τῶν Αἰγυπτίων, καὶ ἔχρησαν αὐτοῖς· καὶ ἐσκύλευσαν τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους ("and the Lord gave favor to his people before the Egyptians, and they lent to them, and they plundered the Egyptians") — σκυλεύω ("plunder, strip") for vayenattselu.

## The Departure from Mitsrayim (vv. 37–42)

**37** And the sons of Yisra'el journeyed from Ra'meses to Sukkot — about six hundred thousand ragli [on foot], the men [ha-gevarim], besides the taf [little-ones].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסְעוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵרַעְמְסֵס סֻכֹּתָה כְּשֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף רַגְלִי הַגְּבָרִים לְבַד מִטָּף (vayis'u venei-Yisra'el me-Ra'meses Sukkotah ke-shesh-me'ot elef ragli ha-gevarim levad mi-taf, "and the sons of Yisra'el journeyed from Ra'meses to Sukkot, about six hundred thousand on foot, the men, besides the little-ones"). **Key terms:** רַעְמְסֵס (Ra'meses) — the starting point, the store-city built by Israelite slave labor (1:11). סֻכֹּת (Sukkot) = "booths" — the first encampment. The number כְּשֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת אֶלֶף (ke-shesh-me'ot elef) = "about six hundred thousand." The prefix כְּ (ke-) = "about, approximately." The word רַגְלִי (ragli) = "on foot, foot-soldier" — from רֶגֶל (regel, "foot"). הַגְּבָרִים (ha-gevarim) = "the men, the able-bodied men" — from גֶּבֶר (gever, "strong man, warrior"). The word טַף (taf) = "little-ones, children" — women are not separately enumerated. LXX: ἀπάραντες δὲ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐκ Ραμεσση εἰς Σοκχωθα εἰς ἑξακοσίας χιλιάδας πεζῶν οἱ ἄνδρες, πλὴν τῆς ἀποσκευῆς.

**38** And also an erev rav [mixed multitude / great mixture] went up with them, and tso'n [flocks] and baqar [herds] — miqneh kaved me'od [very heavy livestock].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְגַם־עֵרֶב רַב עָלָה אִתָּם וְצֹאן וּבָקָר מִקְנֶה כָּבֵד מְאֹד (ve-gam-erev rav alah ittam ve-tso'n u-vaqar miqneh kaved me'od, "and also a mixed multitude, great, went up with them, and flocks and herds — very heavy livestock"). **Key term:** עֵרֶב רַב (erev rav) = "great mixture / mixed multitude" — from ערב (arav, "to mix, mingle") + רַב (rav, "great, many"). This non-Israelite group joins the exodus — Egyptians, other foreigners, perhaps those persuaded by YHWH's power. The word כָּבֵד (kaved) = "heavy" — the root of the hardening vocabulary — here describes the abundance of livestock. LXX: καὶ ἐπίμικτος πολὺς συνανέβη αὐτοῖς καὶ πρόβατα καὶ βόες καὶ κτήνη πολλὰ σφόδρα — ἐπίμικτος ("mixed company") for erev rav.

**39** And they baked the dough which they brought out from Mitsrayim [into] cakes of matstsot [ugot matstsot], for it had not leavened [lo chamets], for they were driven out from Mitsrayim and were not able to delay [le-hitmahmeah], and also they had not made tsedah [provision/food] for themselves.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאפוּ אֶת־הַבָּצֵק אֲשֶׁר הוֹצִיאוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם עֻגֹת מַצּוֹת כִּי לֹא חָמֵץ כִּי־גֹרְשׁוּ מִמִּצְרַיִם וְלֹא יָכְלוּ לְהִתְמַהְמֵהַּ וְגַם־צֵדָה לֹא־עָשׂוּ לָהֶם (vayofu et-ha-batseq asher hotsi'u mi-Mitsrayim ugot matstsot ki lo chamets ki-gorshu mi-Mitsrayim ve-lo yakhlu le-hitmahmeha ve-gam-tsedah lo-asu lahem). The narrative explanation for matstsot: three reasons — (1) the dough had not leavened; (2) they were driven out (גֹרְשׁוּ, gorshu — pual of גרש, garash, fulfilling 11:1); (3) they could not delay. The verb לְהִתְמַהְמֵהַּ (le-hitmahmeha) = hitpael of מהה (mahah, "to linger, delay") — a rare, emphatic form. עֻגֹת (ugot) = "cakes, round bread." LXX: καὶ ἔπεψαν τὸ σταῖς... ἐγκρυφίας ἀζύμους, οὐ γὰρ ἐζυμώθη.

**40** And the dwelling [moshav] of the sons of Yisra'el, which they dwelled in Mitsrayim, [was] thirty years and four hundred years.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמוֹשַׁב בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יָשְׁבוּ בְּמִצְרָיִם שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה (u-moshav benei Yisra'el asher yashvu be-Mitsrayim sheloshim shanah ve-arba me'ot shanah, "and the dwelling of the sons of Yisra'el which they dwelled in Mitsrayim — thirty years and four hundred years"). **Chronological note:** The MT states 430 years in Mitsrayim. LXX differs significantly: καὶ ἡ κατοίκησις τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἣν κατῴκησαν ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ καὶ ἐν γῇ Χανααν ἔτη τετρακόσια τριάκοντα ("and the dwelling of the sons of Israel which they dwelled in the land of Egypt and in the land of Canaan [was] four hundred and thirty years") — LXX adds "and in the land of Canaan," dividing the 430 years between Canaan and Egypt. **41** And it was at the end of thirty years and four hundred years, and it was on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh]: all the hosts [tsiv'ot] of YHWH went out from the land of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה וְאַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שָׁנָה וַיְהִי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה יָצְאוּ כָּל־צִבְאוֹת יְהוָה מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (vayehi mi-qets sheloshim shanah ve-arba me'ot shanah vayehi be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh yats'u kol-tsiv'ot YHWH me-erets Mitsrayim, "and it was at the end of thirty years and four hundred years, and it was on this very day — all the hosts of YHWH went out from the land of Mitsrayim"). The phrase צִבְאוֹת יְהוָה (tsiv'ot YHWH, "the hosts of YHWH") — Yisra'el is now identified as YHWH's army. The repeated phrase בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה (be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh, "on this very day") — emphatic precision, as in v. 17. LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο μετὰ τὰ τετρακόσια τριάκοντα ἔτη ἐξῆλθεν πᾶσα ἡ δύναμις κυρίου ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου — δύναμις ("power, host, army") for tsiv'ot.

**42** It [is] a leil shimmurim [night of watchings/vigils] to YHWH, for bringing them out from the land of Mitsrayim; this [is] that night to YHWH — shimmurim [watchings] for all the sons of Yisra'el throughout their generations.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לֵיל שִׁמֻּרִים הוּא לַיהוָה לְהוֹצִיאָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם הוּא־הַלַּיְלָה הַזֶּה לַיהוָה שִׁמֻּרִים לְכָל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְדֹרֹתָם (leil shimmurim hu la-YHWH le-hotsi'am me-erets Mitsrayim hu-ha-lailah ha-zeh la-YHWH shimmurim le-khol-benei Yisra'el le-dorotam, "a night of watchings it [is] to YHWH for bringing them out from the land of Mitsrayim; it — this night — to YHWH — watchings for all the sons of Yisra'el throughout their generations"). **Key term:** שִׁמֻּרִים (shimmurim) = "watchings, vigils" — an intensive plural from שמר (shamar, "to watch, keep, guard"). The word occurs only here in the OT. A night YHWH kept watch — and a night Yisra'el is to keep watch in perpetuity. The double use of לַיהוָה (la-YHWH, "to YHWH") creates a chiastic emphasis: YHWH watched for Yisra'el; Yisra'el watches for YHWH. LXX: προφυλακή ἐστιν τῷ κυρίῳ ὥστε ἐξαγαγεῖν αὐτοὺς ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου· ἐκείνη ἡ νὺξ αὕτη προφυλακὴ κυρίῳ — προφυλακή ("keeping watch, vigil") for shimmurim.

## Regulations for the Pesach — Who May Eat (vv. 43–51)

**43** And YHWH said to Mosheh and Aharon: "This [is] the chuqqat [statute of] the pesach: no ben-nekhar [son of a foreigner] shall eat of it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן זֹאת חֻקַּת הַפָּסַח כָּל־בֶּן־נֵכָר לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh ve-Aharon zot khuqqat ha-pesakh kol-ben-nekhar lo-yokhal bo). **Key term:** בֶּן־נֵכָר (ben-nekhar) = "son of a foreigner" — from נֵכָר (nekhar, "foreignness, that which is foreign"). This is distinct from גֵּר (ger, "sojourner") — the ben-nekhar is one who has not attached himself to Yisra'el's community or covenant. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν καὶ Ααρων λέγων· οὗτος ὁ νόμος τοῦ πάσχα· πᾶς ἀλλογενὴς οὐκ ἔδεται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — ἀλλογενής ("of another race/foreign-born") for ben-nekhar.

**44** And every eved [slave] of a man, an acquisition of silver [miqnat-khasef] — when you have circumcised him, then he shall eat of it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכָל־עֶבֶד אִישׁ מִקְנַת־כָּסֶף וּמַלְתָּה אֹתוֹ אָז יֹאכַל בּוֹ (ve-khol-eved ish miqnat-khasef u-maltah oto az yokhal bo, "and every slave of a man, an acquisition of silver — when you have circumcised him, then he shall eat of it"). Circumcision is the gate: the purchased slave, once circumcised, enters the covenant community and may eat the pesach. The verb וּמַלְתָּה (u-maltah) from מול (mul, "to circumcise"). LXX: καὶ πᾶν οἰκέτην τινὸς ἢ ἀργυρώνητον περιτεμεῖς αὐτόν, καὶ τότε φάγεται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ.

**45** A toshav [resident] and a sakhir [hired worker] shall not eat of it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads תּוֹשָׁב וְשָׂכִיר לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ (toshav ve-sakhir lo-yokhal bo). **Key terms:** תּוֹשָׁב (toshav) = "resident, temporary dweller" — from ישב (yashav, "to dwell"), with a different social status than ger. שָׂכִיר (sakhir) = "hired worker" — from שכר (sakhar, "to hire"). Neither has entered the covenant through circumcision. LXX: πάροικος ἢ μισθωτὸς οὐκ ἔδεται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ.

**46** In one house it shall be eaten; you shall not bring out from the house any of the flesh outside, and a bone [etsem] you shall not break [tishberu] in it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בְּבַיִת אֶחָד יֵאָכֵל לֹא־תוֹצִיא מִן־הַבַּיִת מִן־הַבָּשָׂר חוּצָה וְעֶצֶם לֹא תִשְׁבְּרוּ־בוֹ (be-vayit ekhad ye'akhel lo-totsi min-ha-bayit min-ha-basar khutsah ve-etsem lo tishberu-vo, "in one house it shall be eaten; you shall not bring out from the house any of the flesh outside; and a bone you shall not break in it"). **THEOLOGICALLY SIGNIFICANT — the unbroken bone:** The prohibition וְעֶצֶם לֹא תִשְׁבְּרוּ־בוֹ (ve-etsem lo tishberu-vo, "and a bone you shall not break in it") preserves the wholeness of the sacrifice. The word עֶצֶם (etsem) = "bone" — the same word as in v. 41 meaning "substance, very [day]." The pesach is eaten in one house, none taken outside, and its skeletal structure remains intact — a whole offering consumed by a whole community in a whole house. This is cited in the NT (John 19:36) regarding the crucifixion. LXX: ἐν οἰκίᾳ μιᾷ βρωθήσεται, καὶ οὐκ ἐξοίσετε ἐκ τῆς οἰκίας τῶν κρεῶν ἔξω, καὶ ὀστοῦν οὐ συντρίψετε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — ὀστοῦν ("bone") for etsem; συντρίβω ("break, shatter") for shavar.

**47** All the congregation [adat] of Yisra'el shall do [keep] it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כָּל־עֲדַת יִשְׂרָאֵל יַעֲשׂוּ אֹתוֹ (kol-adat Yisra'el ya'asu oto). The entire community participates — the pesach is not optional but obligatory for all of adat Yisra'el. LXX: πᾶσα συναγωγὴ υἱῶν Ισραηλ ποιήσει αὐτό.

**48** And when a ger [sojourner] sojourns with you and would make the pesach to YHWH — every male of his shall be circumcised, and then he shall draw near to make it; and he shall be as a native of the land [ke-ezrach ha-arets]. And no uncircumcised [arel] shall eat of it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכִי־יָגוּר אִתְּךָ גֵּר וְעָשָׂה פֶסַח לַיהוָה הִמּוֹל לוֹ כָל־זָכָר וְאָז יִקְרַב לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ וְהָיָה כְּאֶזְרַח הָאָרֶץ וְכָל־עָרֵל לֹא־יֹאכַל בּוֹ (ve-khi-yagur ittekha ger ve-asah fesakh la-YHWH himmol lo khol-zakhar ve-az yiqrav la-asoto ve-hayah ke-ezrakh ha-arets ve-khol-arel lo-yokhal bo). **Key principle — inclusion through circumcision:** The ger who circumcises all his males may participate fully and becomes כְּאֶזְרַח הָאָרֶץ (ke-ezrakh ha-arets, "as a native of the land"). The word עָרֵל (arel) = "uncircumcised" — from ערל. Circumcision is the covenant boundary, not ethnicity. The verb יִקְרַב (yiqrav) from קרב (qarav, "to draw near, approach") — a cultic term for approaching sacred things. LXX: ἐὰν δέ τις προσέλθῃ πρὸς ὑμᾶς προσήλυτος ποιῆσαι τὸ πάσχα κυρίῳ, περιτεμεῖς αὐτοῦ πᾶν ἀρσενικόν, καὶ τότε προσελεύσεται ποιῆσαι αὐτό, καὶ ἔσται ὥσπερ καὶ ὁ αὐτόχθων τῆς γῆς — προσήλυτος ("proselyte") for ger; αὐτόχθων ("native-born") for ezrakh.

**49** Torah achat [one torah/law] shall be for the native [la-ezrakh] and for the ger [sojourner] who sojourns in your midst."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads תּוֹרָה אַחַת יִהְיֶה לָאֶזְרָח וְלַגֵּר הַגָּר בְּתוֹכְכֶם (torah akhat yihyeh la-ezrakh ve-la-ger ha-gar be-tokhekhem, "one torah shall be for the native and for the sojourner who sojourns in your midst"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — torah achat:** One law for native and sojourner — a foundational principle of legal equality. The word תּוֹרָה (torah) here means "instruction, law, directive" — not the Torah as a whole corpus, but a specific ruling. This is the earliest and most explicit statement of legal equality between Israelite and non-Israelite in the Pentateuch. LXX: νόμος εἷς ἔσται τῷ ἐγχωρίῳ καὶ τῷ προσελθόντι προσηλύτῳ ἐν ὑμῖν — νόμος εἷς ("one law") for torah akhat.

**50** And all the sons of Yisra'el did; as YHWH commanded Mosheh and Aharon, so they did.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּעֲשׂוּ כָּל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה וְאֶת־אַהֲרֹן כֵּן עָשׂוּ (vaya'asu kol-benei Yisra'el ka-asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh ve-et-Aharon ken asu). The compliance formula repeated from v. 28 — bookending the chapter with obedience. LXX: καὶ ἐποίησαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ καθὰ ἐνετείλατο κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ καὶ Ααρων, οὕτως ἐποίησαν.

**51** And it was on this very day [be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh]: YHWH brought out the sons of Yisra'el from the land of Mitsrayim by their hosts [al-tsiv'otam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בְּעֶצֶם הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם עַל־צִבְאֹתָם (vayehi be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh hotsi YHWH et-benei Yisra'el me-erets Mitsrayim al-tsiv'otam, "and it was on this very day — YHWH brought out the sons of Yisra'el from the land of Mitsrayim by their hosts"). The closing verse echoes v. 41 and v. 17 with the emphatic be-etsem ha-yom ha-zeh. The verb הוֹצִיא (hotsi) = hiphil of יצא (yatsa, "to go out") — "He caused to go out, He brought out." YHWH is the subject: He brought them out. The phrase עַל־צִבְאֹתָם (al-tsiv'otam, "by/according to their hosts") — organized, marshalled, in military formation. Yisra'el departs not as a refugee stream but as YHWH's mobilized army. LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ἐξήγαγεν κύριος τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου σὺν δυνάμει αὐτῶν.

## Key Lexical Notes — Exodus 12

- **pesach** (פֶּסַח, vv. 11, 21, 27, 43, 48): "passover" — from פסח (pasakh). The core verb of the chapter. Its semantic range includes "pass over, skip, protect, guard." In v. 13 YHWH "passes over" the blood-marked houses; in v. 23 He "stands guard over the door." The noun denotes both the sacrifice (the pesach-lamb) and the festival. The LXX transliterates as πάσχα (pascha) rather than translating — recognizing it as a proper name for this unique institution.

- **seh** (שֶׂה, vv. 3–5): "a head of small livestock" — deliberately ambiguous between lamb (keves) and goat-kid (ez). The broader term allows choice; v. 5 specifies either is acceptable. The seh must be tamim (unblemished), zakhar (male), ben-shanah (a year old).

- **matstsot / matstsah** (מַצּוֹת / מַצָּה, vv. 8, 15, 17–20, 39): "unleavened bread" — bread made without se'or (leaven). The narrative in v. 34/39 provides the etiological origin: the dough had no time to rise. But the command in vv. 15–20 is prescriptive, not merely memorial — the absence of leaven is commanded before the hasty departure, suggesting a theological meaning beyond historical memory.

- **chamets** (חָמֵץ, vv. 15, 19–20, 34, 39): "leavened bread / that which is leavened" — from the verb חמץ (khamats, "to be sour, ferment, leaven"). The prohibition is absolute during the seven days of matstsot, with the penalty of karet (excision from the community) for violation.

- **mashchit** (מַשְׁחִית, vv. 13, 23): "destroyer" — from שחת (shakhat, "to destroy, ruin, corrupt"). The agent of death that YHWH unleashes upon Mitsrayim but from which He actively protects the blood-marked houses. Whether this is an angelic being, a personified force, or a metaphor for YHWH's own destructive action is debated. Verse 23 distinguishes YHWH (who passes over) from the mashchit (who is not permitted to enter).

- **shimmurim** (שִׁמֻּרִים, v. 42): "watchings, vigils" — an intensive plural from shamar. A hapax legomenon (unique occurrence). YHWH's watchful night of deliverance becomes Yisra'el's perpetual night of watchful commemoration.

- **erev rav** (עֵרֶב רַב, v. 38): "great mixture / mixed multitude" — the non-Israelite group that joined the exodus. Their presence raises the question of inclusion that vv. 43–49 address: who may eat the pesach? The answer is circumcision, not ethnicity.

- **chipazon** (חִפָּזוֹן, v. 11): "haste, trepidation" — one of the rarest words in the OT (3 occurrences total). The haste of the departure is not joyful but anxious — the urgency of fleeing before the destroyer, of eating with loins girded and sandals on.

- **torah achat** (תּוֹרָה אַחַת, v. 49): "one law" — the principle that native and sojourner stand under the same legal obligation regarding the pesach. This is a foundational statement of legal equality that will be expanded throughout the Torah (Lev 24:22, Num 15:15–16).

- **bekhor** (בְּכוֹר, vv. 12, 29): "firstborn" — the death of every firstborn in Mitsrayim, from Par'oh's throne to the dungeon, constitutes the decisive plague. The firstborn represents the future, the heir, the family's continuation. YHWH strikes at Egypt's future itself.

- **shefatim** (שְׁפָטִים, v. 12): "judgments" — YHWH executes judgments against the gods of Mitsrayim. The plagues are not merely punitive against humans but constitute a divine lawsuit against the Egyptian pantheon, exposing the impotence of every deity before YHWH.

## Hardening-Verb Note — Exodus 12

No hardening verbs appear in Exodus 12. The hardening sequence concluded at 11:10. However, note the ironic use of חזק (chazaq) in v. 33: וַתֶּחֱזַק מִצְרַיִם עַל־הָעָם (vateckezaq Mitsrayim al-ha-am, "Mitsrayim was urgent/strong upon the people") — the same root that described Par'oh's hardened resistance now describes Egypt's desperate urgency to expel Yisra'el. The verb that held Yisra'el captive now drives them free.
